journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Identification of immunostimulatory antigens in Group A Streptococcus–derived vesicles
abstract: An effective vaccine against Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Streptococcus, GAS) is urgently needed. Bacteria-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) are emerging as promising vaccine platforms. In this study, we evaluated the immunostimulatory properties of GAS-derived EVs when administered intranasally in mice. Immunization induced a strong humoral response, including pathogen-specific immunoglobulin G (IgG) and immunoglobulin A (IgA) antibodies. Additionally, we observed local and systemic T cell responses, specifically a robust Th17 response along with a modest but statistically significant Th1 response. Through immunoprecipitation coupled with mass spectrometry and western blotting, we identified seven immunogenic proteins, including the lipoproteins MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, MalX, and the glutamine transporter GlnP. These antigens were recognized by human sera from both healthy individuals and patients with necrotizing soft tissue infection. Finally, we demonstrate that these antigens stimulate production and secretion of IL-17A by lung cells and splenocytes and that they are highly conserved across diverse GAS M-types, highlighting their potential as broadly protective vaccine candidates.
discussion: DiscussionGroup A streptococcal diseases represent a significant burden for public health, however, there is currently no licensed vaccine against this pathogen. In this work, we examined the immunogenicity of GAS-derived EVs. Our findings demonstrate that GAS EVs are potent immunostimulants that elicit local and systemic humoral and T cell responses upon intranasal delivery in mice. GAS EVs served as an antigen presenting platform that allowed identification of the immunostimulatory antigens MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, MalX, and GlnP. These antigens elicit IgA, IgG, and IL-17A production in mice, are relevant in humans, and are highly conserved across different GAS M-types, highlighting their potential as broadly protective vaccine candidates.Protective immunity to GAS infections is not well understood, however, it is likely to require the presence of pathogen specific antibodies that can promote opsonophagocytosis, neutralization of toxins/virulence factors and blocking of bacterial adhesion together with a CD4+ T cell response . Accumulating evidence suggest that induction of mucosal Th17 and antibody responses can protect from S. pyogenes infections , probably by IL-17A mediated recruitment and expansion of neutrophils and macrophages, key cells in GAS clearance . In this work, we demonstrate that intranasal immunization with alum-adjuvated GAS EVs induces a strong mucosal response, characterized by production of IgA and IL-17A producing CD4+ T cells. Additionally, it elicits the production of opsonizing IgG antibodies in sera and a robust systemic Th17 response. A similar, local and systemic, Th17 response was also observed after immunization with GAS EVs in the absence of adjuvant, confirming their inherent adjuvant properties. Together, our data highlight the potency of GAS EVs as immunostimulants and their potential role in eliciting protective immunity against mucosal and systemic GAS infections.Th1 responses are also important for protection against GAS infections, and these may play a key role in fighting invasive diseases, while Th17 responses may be more relevant against mucosal infections. In fact, it was previously shown that intranasal inoculation with heat killed GAS induced IL-17A-producing T cells, while intravenous and subcutaneous challenge induced IFNγ-producing cells . Moreover, two independent studies showed that GAS vaccines can protect from invasive disease when formulated with Th1-promoting adjuvants . Although the T cell response we observed upon intranasal immunization with alum-adjuvated GAS EVs was dominantly Th17, we also found a small, but significant increase in the production of IFNγ. Importantly, IFNγ production was higher in lung and spleen cells from mice immunized with EVs in the absence of alum, which preferentially induces a Th2-like response . It is therefore possible that intranasal immunization with EVs alone can induce an immune response protective against invasive disease.EVs have emerged as a novel vaccine platform against bacterial infections, not only due to their potent immunostimulatory and self-adjuvating properties but also due to the display of multiple antigens in their native conformation . In this work, GAS EVs served as an antigen presenting platform that allowed identification of the highly conserved immunostimulatory antigens MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, and GlnP. Although MalX was not found in GAS EVs, it was included due to its role in EV-mediated protection against invasive pneumococcal disease . Of the seven antigens, six are lipoproteins involved in central metabolic processes. PrsA1 and PrsA2 are parvulin-type peptidyl-prolyl isomerases required to maintain the GAS proteome homeostasis by assisting in the folding and maturation of extracellular proteins . MtsA, BMP, MetQ, and MalX are substrate-binding lipoproteins of ABC transporters involved in the uptake of iron, zinc, and manganese [MtsA, ], ribonucleosides [BMP, ], methionine [MetQ, ] and maltose [MalX, ]. GlnP is the product of Spy_1315, which encodes for an ABC transporter permease subunit involved in L-glutamine transport. GlnP shares high similarity with parts of the ABC transporter involved in lysine, arginine, and ornithine transport in Streptococcus infantarius, suggesting GlnP may also be involved in L-lysine transport, which is essential for bacterial growth . Previous work demonstrated that protein lipidation enhances immunogenicity and protective efficacy . Here, we show that EV-associated lipoproteins are recognized better than recombinant proteins lacking signal peptide and lipidation when probed with sera from GAS-EV immunized mice. This suggests that antigen delivery in EVs plays a key role in immunogenicity.In this study, MetQ, MalX, and GlnP are reported as vaccine candidates in GAS. These three proteins were here shown to induce IL-17A production by lung cells and splenocytes from EV-vaccinated mice and are therefore good Th17 antigens. Additionally, antibodies against MetQ, MalX, and especially GlnP were found in sera from healthy individuals and NSTI patients. Of the seven antigens tested here, GlnP was the most immunogenic in mice and humans, which together with its key role in bacterial growth highlights its potential as a vaccine candidate.MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, and PrsA2 have previously been identified as potential vaccine candidates . Immunization with BMP, either alone or in combination with other antigens (Spy7 vaccine) protected against skin infection or bacterial dissemination, respectively . Similarly, immunization with PrsA1 and PrsA2 conferred protection against invasive challenge . Our data support the role of MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, and PrsA2 as vaccine candidates. BMP was strongly recognized by sera from healthy individuals and NSTI patients, induced production of high levels of IL-17A in lung cells and splenocytes from EV-vaccinated mice and was the only antigen capable of stimulating IFNγ-production by splenocytes.In conclusion, our work highlights the potential of using EVs including MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, MalX, and GlnP as vaccine candidates. These membrane associated antigens play key roles in bacterial growth, nutrient uptake, biofilm formation, oxidative stress resistance, survival in murine macrophages and virulence . Additionally, these antigens are highly conserved across different M-types, are relevant in humans, and capable of eliciting immune responses associated with protection.
figure_legends: Intranasal immunization with alum-adjuvated GAS EVs generates GAS-specific antibodies. (A) Immunization scheme. 5-wk-old male C57BL/6 mice were immunized intranasally with 50 µL of GAS EVs (10 µg) formulated with aluminum hydroxide (Alum, 10 mg/mL). The control group was immunized with alum in PBS. (B) GAS-specific IgG and IgA levels in serum and nasal wash, respectively, were analyzed by whole-bacteria ELISA (plates were coated with heat-inactivated GAS 5448 AP). Each symbol represents data from an individual mouse. (C) Survival of GAS 5448 following opsonophagocytic assay with HL-60 cells. Pooled sera from five independent experiments were tested. Bars represent mean ± SEM of three independent experiments. Significance was determined by the two-tailed unpaired t test (IgG and bacterial survival %) or Mann–Whitney test (IgA). **P ≤ 0.01; ***P ≤ 0.001.

Intranasal immunization with alum-adjuvated GAS EVs elicits Th17 and Th1 responses in the lungs. Mice were immunized as described in Fig. 1A. Lungs were collected 7 d after the last immunization and stained for flow cytometry analysis or cultured ex vivo to measure cytokines response to antigen stimulation. Representative (A) histogram and percentage (%) of RORγT+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. Representative (B) flow cytometry plots, percentage (%) and total number (#) of IL-17+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. Lung cells were cultured ex vivo and stimulated with GAS EVs or media for 72 h; secretion of (C) Th17-specific IL-17A was measured by ELISA. Representative (D) histogram and percentage (%) of T-bet+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. Representative (E) flow cytometry plots, percentage (%) and total number (#) of IFNγ+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. (F) Quantification of Th1-specific IFNγ secreted into the culture supernatant upon stimulation with GAS EVs or media for 72h. Cytokine data are normalized to media stimulation controls. Bars represent mean ± SEM of two (flow cytometry) or three (cytokines) independent experiments. Each symbol represents data from an individual mouse. Significance was determined by the two- tailed unpaired t test (% and # of IL-17+ T cells, % of IFNγ+ and RORγT+ T cells) or Mann–Whitney test (# of IFNγ+ T cells, % of T-bet+ T cells and IL-17A and IFNγ secretion). **P ≤ 0.01; ***P ≤ 0.001; ****P ≤ 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Intranasal immunization with alum-adjuvated GAS EVs induces a T cell response in the spleen. Mice were immunized as described in Fig. 1A. Spleen samples were collected 7 d after the last immunization. Representative (A) flow cytometry plots, percentage (%) and total number (#) of IL-17+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. (B) Splenocytes were stimulated for 72h with GAS EVs or media, before secreted IL-17A was quantified in the supernatant. Representative (C) flow cytometry plots, percentage (%) and total number (#) of IFNγ+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells. (D) Quantification of IFNγ secreted into the culture supernatant upon splenocyte stimulation with GAS EVs or media for 72h. Cytokine data are normalized to media stimulation controls. Bars represent mean ± SEM of two (flow cytometry) or three (cytokines) independent experiments. Each symbol represents data from an individual mouse. Significance was determined by the two- tailed unpaired t test (# of IL-17+ T cells, % and # of IFNγ+ T cells and IFNγ secretion) or Mann–Whitney test (% of IL-17+ T cells and IL- 17A secretion). *P ≤ 0.05; ***P ≤ 0.001; ****P ≤ 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Intranasal immunization with GAS EVs alone elicits Th17 and Th1 responses in the lungs and spleen. Mice were immunized twice with 10 µg of EVs following the immunization scheme described in Fig. 1A. Lung and spleen cells were collected 7 d after the last immunization and stained for flow cytometry analysis following nonspecific restimulation with PMA and ionomycin. (A and B) Percentage (%) and total number (#) of IL-17+ CD44+ CD4+ in lungs and spleen samples, and (C and D) percentage (%) and total number (#) of IFNγ+ CD44+ CD4+ T cells in lungs and spleen samples. Bars represent mean ± SEM of one experiment. Each symbol represents data from an individual mouse. Significance was determined by the two-tailed unpaired t test. *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P ≤ 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Identification of immunostimulatory antigens in GAS EVs. (A) Two and five µg of GAS 5448 AP EVs were loaded on an SDS-PAGE and stained with Coomassie (Left). Western blot analysis of GAS EVs (2 µg) using sera from EV+ alum immunized mice (Right). (B) Antigen-specific IgG levels in sera vs. recombinantly expressed MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, GlnP, and MalX were measured by ELISA. Bars represent mean ± SEM. Statistical comparisons were made using the Mann–Whitney test. *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P ≤ 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Immunostimulatory antigens identified in GAS EVs react with human plasma. (A) Western blot against recombinantly expressed MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, GlnP, and MalX (2 µg of each protein, see SI Appendix, Fig. S3 for Coomassie) using commercial human AB serum (Merck, H4522). (B) Antigen-specific IgG levels in plasma from patients with NSTI vs. recombinantly expressed MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, GlnP, and MalX were measured by ELISA. Samples incubated without human plasma were used as negative controls (Ctrl −). Bars represent mean ± SEM. Each symbol represents data from an individual patient. Statistical comparisons were made using the Mann–Whitney test (PrsA1 and MetQ) or two-tailed unpaired t test (MtsA, BMP, PrsA2, GlnP, and MalX). *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ns, not significant.

IL-17A response to antigen stimulation. (A) Lung cells and (B) splenocytes from mice immunized with alum or GAS EVs formulated with alum were collected 1 wk after the second immunization. Cells were cultured ex vivo and stimulated with 1 µg of recombinantly expressed proteins (MtsA, BMP, PrsA1, PrsA2, MetQ, GlnP, or MalX) or media for 72 h. The concentration of IL-17A in culture supernatants was measured by ELISA. Data are normalized to media stimulation controls and represented as mean ± SEM of two independent experiments. Statistical comparisons were made using the Mann–Whitney test. **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P ≤ 0.0001.

Vaccine candidates identified in GAS EVs are conserved across GAS strains. (A) Western blot analysis of GAS EVs and bacterial lysates of different invasive GAS isolates (M-type is indicated in brackets). Samples were probed with sera from GAS EVs-immunized mice (Top panel) or anti-GAPDH as a loading control (Bottom panel). (B) IgG binding to different GAS strains was analyzed by whole-cell ELISA. Plates were coated with different invasive GAS strains, covering a range of M-types, and probed with sera from mice vaccinated with alum (light gray circles) or GAS EVs formulated with alum (blue circles). Bars represent mean ± SEM of four independent experiments. Each symbol represents data from an individual mouse. Significance was determined by the two-tailed unpaired t test (S073, S221, S338, and S530) or Mann–Whitney test (AP, S011, S137, and S150). ****P ≤ 0.0001.
